title: Correcting A Failing BVM Seal
stem: An unresponsive 58-year-old is being carried down a narrow stairwell. Snoring respirations persist and the one-person bag-valve-mask seal is leaking. What should the paramedic do next?

options.B: Move, reposition, and reseal the mask
options.D: Place an SGA and continue movement

rationale: A is best because the immediate problem is ineffective ventilation from poor airway position and mask seal; stopping movement, opening the airway, adding an adjunct, and using a two-person BVM corrects the priority problem. B delays correction of ventilation. C measures ventilation without fixing it. D escalates before optimizing basic ventilation.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:30. Locator: Primary Source Materials/Textbooks/Third Edition_ Emergency Medical Services_ Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight 3rd.pdf; Ch 3, p 30, PDF p 48.
generation_notes: Paramedic next-action experiment converted to production format on 2026-07-06; distractors emphasize correct-later priority/sequence errors. title: Airway Choice After CPR Pauses
stem: A cardiac arrest patient has continuous CPR in progress. Two laryngoscopy attempts have caused long pauses in compressions. What should the paramedic do next?

options.D: Move to the ambulance for airway setup

rationale: C is best because repeated intubation attempts are interrupting CPR, and an SGA can provide ventilation while preserving compression continuity. A repeats the failed strategy. B sacrifices compression continuity. D moves the airway problem instead of solving it.
citation: Jarvis JL, Panchal AR, Lyng JW, et al. Evidence-Based Guideline for Prehospital Airway Management. Prehosp Emerg Care. 2024;28(4):545-557. doi:10.1080/10903127.2023.2281363; Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:136. title: Post-Tonsillectomy Airway Priority
stem: A 9-year-old is spitting bright red blood 5 days after tonsillectomy and has gurgling respirations. What should the paramedic do next?
rationale: B is best because blood and secretions are the immediate airway threat; drainage positioning, suction, oxygen, and airway contingency come ahead of access, consultation, or transport alone. A, C, and D are plausible supporting actions, but none clears the airway threat.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:46. title: Compensated Shock With Bleeding
stem: A 24-year-old with a thigh laceration is pale. BP is 118/74, HR is 128, and blood is soaking through a towel. What should the paramedic do next?
rationale: D is best because compensated shock can exist despite a normal systolic pressure, and hemorrhage control with ongoing reassessment is the next priority. A gathers more data. B starts resuscitation access. C changes location. None addresses bleeding first.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:73. title: Obstructive Shock From Chest Trauma
stem: A restrained driver with blunt chest trauma has absent breath sounds on the left and BP 78/50. What should the paramedic do next?

options.B: Establish IV access and give a fluid bolus
options.D: Begin transport to the trauma center

rationale: A is best because hypotension with unilateral absent breath sounds after chest trauma is a field indication to decompress suspected tension pneumothorax. B, C, and D are plausible trauma actions, but they do not relieve the obstructive cause.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:75. topic: Placement of intraosseous lines (adult and pediatric)
title: Pediatric IO Site With Tibial Fracture
stem: A critically ill 4-year-old needs immediate vascular access after two failed IV attempts. The right proximal tibia is fractured and deformed; the left proximal tibia is intact. Which IO site should the paramedic choose next?
rationale: B is best because IO contraindications are site-specific; a fractured tibia should be avoided while the intact contralateral proximal tibia is an available pediatric IO site. A uses the injured bone. C is not the preferred young-child site when a standard tibial site is available and may be device- or protocol-limited. D is not a typical EMS IO access site.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:86. title: Airway First In Altered Mental Status
stem: A 72-year-old is found unresponsive with slow, shallow respirations. What should the paramedic do first?
rationale: C is best because airway and ventilation are first in an unresponsive patient with slow, shallow respirations. A, B, and D may matter later, but they do not correct the immediate ventilation problem.

topic: Management and resuscitation of multi-trauma patient
title: Trauma Tension Pneumothorax Priority
stem: A motorcyclist has hypotension and unilateral absent breath sounds after blunt chest trauma. What should the paramedic do next?

options.B: Apply oxygen and package for transport

rationale: D is best because traumatic tension pneumothorax with hypotension and unilateral absent breath sounds requires immediate decompression. A, B, and C are real trauma actions, but they do not treat the life threat.

title: Junctional Hemorrhage Packing
stem: A patient has deep groin bleeding after a stabbing. Direct pressure slows but does not control the bleeding. What should the paramedic do next?
rationale: A is best because junctional hemorrhage not controlled by pressure should be packed with gauze or a hemostatic dressing and held with firm pressure. B persists with an inadequate method. C supports circulation without controlling the loss. D uses a limb-tourniquet strategy for a groin wound.

title: Alkali Eye Burn Irrigation
stem: A factory worker splashes liquid alkali into both eyes. What should the paramedic do next?

options.C: Call poison control and review SDS

rationale: B is best because alkali eye exposure requires immediate copious irrigation; time matters more than exam detail or chemical identification. A, C, and D are reasonable supporting steps, but irrigation is the immediate action.

title: Crush Release Preparation
stem: A worker's leg has been trapped under concrete for 4 hours, and rescuers are ready to lift the slab. What should the paramedic do next?
rationale: C is best because prolonged crush can release potassium, acid, and myoglobin when the weight is lifted, so monitoring, vascular access if possible, and resuscitation should be prepared before release. A, B, and D underprepare for reperfusion effects.

topic: Use of capnometry and capnography waveforms in diagnosis and management
title: Capnography Drop During Ventilation
stem: An intubated patient is being ventilated during transport. Waveform capnography suddenly drops, but pulse oximetry remains 96%. What should the paramedic do next?
rationale: D is best because capnography changes can reveal ventilation, circuit, tube, or patient deterioration before pulse oximetry falls. A, B, and C respond around the problem instead of checking the ventilation system.

topic: Management of respiratory distress or shortness of breath
title: Ventilatory Support In COPD Exhaustion
stem: A 67-year-old with COPD is exhausted and has shallow respirations despite oxygen. What should the paramedic do next?
rationale: A is best because exhaustion with shallow respirations signals ventilatory failure; ventilatory support is the priority while reversible causes are treated. B, C, and D are plausible steps that do not correct ventilation.

stem: A 59-year-old with pulmonary edema is awake, cooperative, and still hypoxemic on a nonrebreather. What should the paramedic do next?

options.A: Start IV access and diuretic therapy

rationale: B is best because an awake, cooperative pulmonary-edema patient who remains hypoxemic on a nonrebreather is a good CPAP candidate. A, C, and D are plausible preparations, but CPAP is the next respiratory intervention.

title: Recurrent Tension Pneumothorax
stem: Ten minutes after needle decompression for tension pneumothorax, hypotension and absent breath sounds return on the same side. What should the paramedic do next?

options.A: Give fluid bolus and reassess BP

rationale: C is best because recurrent hypotension and absent breath sounds after decompression suggest reaccumulation or catheter failure. A, B, and D address other concerns without checking the failed decompression pathway.

title: Insulin Pump Hypoglycemia
stem: A patient with an insulin pump is confused. EMS glucose is 42 mg/dL, and the patient cannot swallow safely. What should the paramedic do next?

options.A: Suspend pump and give glucose safely
options.C: Establish IV access and start transport
options.D: Confirm pump history and recent meals

rationale: A is best because the patient has active hypoglycemia and cannot swallow safely, so insulin delivery should be stopped or suspended if able while glucose is given by a safe route. B and D gather device or history details without treating hypoglycemia. C starts access and transport without correcting the glucose.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:189-190. title: CGM Conflict With EMS Glucose
stem: A teenager with a continuous glucose monitor is confused. The monitor app shows 94 mg/dL, but the EMS glucometer reads 39 mg/dL. What should the paramedic do next?

options.B: Treat using the EMS glucose result
options.C: Use the monitor trend to decide on dextrose
options.D: Trust the monitor app and start transport

rationale: B is best because symptomatic hypoglycemia should be treated using the EMS glucose result and clinical picture. A, C, and D give extra weight to device confirmation while the patient remains hypoglycemic.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:189-190. title: Dialysis Fistula Hemorrhage
stem: A dialysis patient has brisk bleeding from an arm fistula. What should the paramedic do next?

options.A: Apply bulky dressing and elevate arm

title: Hyperkalemia With ECG Changes
stem: A missed-dialysis patient has wide QRS complexes and peaked T waves on ECG. What should the paramedic do next under an ALS hyperkalemia protocol?

title: Placenta Previa Exam Avoidance
stem: A 34-week pregnant patient reports sudden painless bright red vaginal bleeding. What should the paramedic do next?

options.A: Prepare pelvic exam and IV access
options.C: Estimate blood loss and trend vitals

rationale: B is best because painless third-trimester bleeding raises concern for placenta previa, so vaginal examination should be avoided and the patient transported for obstetric evaluation. A risks provoking hemorrhage. C and D collect useful information but do not change the field priority.

title: Neonatal Positive-Pressure Ventilation
stem: A term newborn remains apneic after drying and stimulation. HR is 80/min. What should the paramedic do next?

options.A: Continue stimulation and drying for another minute
options.C: Obtain pulse oximetry for oxygen targeting

rationale: D is best because apnea after drying and stimulation with heart rate below 100 requires positive-pressure ventilation. A, B, and C do not establish effective ventilation.

title: Ventilation Before Naloxone
stem: A patient with suspected opioid overdose has RR 4. A BVM and naloxone are available. What is the best immediate approach?

options.B: Start IV access and give naloxone
options.D: Load and give naloxone en route

rationale: A is best because ventilatory failure is the immediate life threat in opioid overdose; current NAEMSP OUD guidance emphasizes strong BLS and excellent BVM ventilation, while naloxone should be titrated to adequate respirations and airway protection. B, C, and D put medication goals or movement ahead of ventilation.

title: Atropine Endpoint In Organophosphate Poisoning
stem: A farm worker exposed to pesticide has wheezing and copious secretions. What should guide atropine treatment?

options.A: Heart rate above 100/min and stable BP
options.D: Improved mental status and calmer breathing

rationale: B is best because atropine for organophosphate poisoning is titrated to drying secretions and easier ventilation. A, C, and D are unreliable treatment endpoints.

title: Toxicologic Seizure Treatment
stem: A patient overdosed on an unknown stimulant and is actively seizing. What should the paramedic do next?

options.A: Obtain ECG and check QRS width
options.D: Prepare charcoal and call poison control

rationale: C is best because toxicologic seizures are treated first-line with parenteral benzodiazepines. A, B, and D are poisoning-related actions, but they do not stop the seizure.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:376. title: Recognizing Prehospital Septic Shock
stem: An older adult with suspected pneumonia is febrile, confused, and hypotensive. What should the paramedic do next?
rationale: D is best because suspected infection with fever, altered mental status, and hypotension suggests septic shock requiring protocol care and transport. A, B, and C focus on secondary data or symptom treatment rather than shock care.
citation: Pinto-Villalba RS, Balseca-Arellano D, Leon-Rojas JE. Septic shock in the prehospital setting: a scoping review. Scand J Trauma Resusc Emerg Med. 2024;32:113. title: Oxygenation In Septic Shock
stem: A septic patient has SpO2 90% on room air. Oxygen is available. What should the paramedic do next?
rationale: A is best because the patient is hypoxemic and needs oxygen titrated with ongoing ventilation and perfusion monitoring. B and C are incomplete priorities. D leaves hypoxemia untreated.
citation: Pinto-Villalba RS, Balseca-Arellano D, Leon-Rojas JE. Septic shock in the prehospital setting: a scoping review. Scand J Trauma Resusc Emerg Med. 2024;32:113. title: Tuberculosis Airborne Precautions
stem: A clinic asks EMS to transport a patient with suspected pulmonary tuberculosis. What should the crew do before close contact and transport?

options.A: Perform lung exam, vitals, chart review, paperwork
options.B: Use N95s, mask patient, ventilate, notify
options.C: Load patient, open windows, don respirators, transport
options.D: Mask patient, gloves, standard precautions, transport

rationale: B is best because suspected pulmonary tuberculosis requires crew respiratory protection, patient source control if tolerated, ventilation precautions, and appropriate notification. A, C, and D under-protect the crew or delay source control.
citation: MacDonald RD. Infectious and communicable diseases. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. stem: A patient with fever, rash, and respiratory symptoms is being transported to the ED. PPE and patient masking are already in place. What should the paramedic do next?

options.A: Complete written report for receiving facility

rationale: C is best because advance notification lets the receiving facility prepare isolation and PPE. A, B, and D are real documentation or cleanup tasks, but they miss the prearrival notification need.

title: Direct Suicide Risk Questions
stem: A patient with depression says, "I just do not want to be here anymore." What should the paramedic do next?

options.B: Ask family about risk and weapons

rationale: D is best because direct questioning about suicidal thoughts, plan, means, and safety is indicated and does not create suicidality. A, B, and C gather useful information or move care forward without asking the patient directly.

title: Panic Mimic Assessment
stem: A 21-year-old is breathing rapidly after an argument and reports chest tightness. SpO2 is 99%. Friends say this is "just panic." What should the paramedic do next?

options.A: Assess medical causes and calm the patient
options.B: Ask friends about prior panic episodes
options.D: Offer refusal paperwork if symptoms seem panic-related

rationale: A is best because apparent panic should still be assessed for organic, toxic, metabolic, traumatic, and cardiac causes while calming the patient. B anchors on panic history too early. C treats presumed anxiety. D moves to disposition before medical assessment.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:471-472. title: Weapon Risk In Suicidal Patient
stem: A patient says he wants to die. Family reports a loaded handgun may be in the room. What should the crew do next?

options.A: Continue verbal contact from the doorway
options.C: Ask family to secure the firearm
options.D: Enter with the stretcher ready for transport

rationale: B is best because a possible firearm makes scene safety and coordinated law-enforcement involvement the immediate priority. A, C, and D keep EMS or family engaged with an uncontrolled weapon risk.

title: Agitated Delirium As Medical Emergency
stem: After medication per protocol, a stimulant-intoxicated patient is controlled but still hot to touch. What should the paramedic do next?
rationale: C is best because stimulant-associated agitated delirium with hyperthermia is a medical emergency; once safety and medication are addressed, cooling should not wait for transport or documentation. A, B, and D do not treat the ongoing physiologic threat.

title: Urban Hypothermia Assessment
stem: An older adult from a poorly heated apartment remains confused after basic warming has started. What should the paramedic do next?
rationale: D is best because urban hypothermia may reflect illness, medication effects, glucose abnormality, or social vulnerability; once basic warming has started, persistent confusion needs medical assessment. A and C continue warming without the broader assessment. B is not the next clinical action.

title: Gentle Handling In Moderate Hypothermia
stem: A confused hypothermic patient pulled from cold water wants to walk to the ambulance. What should the paramedic do next?
rationale: A is best because moderate hypothermia requires horizontal positioning, gentle handling, wet-clothing removal, insulation, and active warming as available. B, C, and D do not prioritize core protection and gentle handling.

title: Cool First In Exertional Heat Stroke
stem: A collapsed runner is confused and hot after a race. A cold-water immersion tub is available at the medical tent. What should the paramedic do next?

options.C: Move the patient to the air-conditioned ambulance
options.D: Obtain a full set of vital signs and history

rationale: B is best because exertional heat stroke requires immediate rapid cooling when effective cooling is available. A, C, and D are real scene actions that lose time from cooling.

title: Ventilation Priority In Drowning Arrest
stem: A child is pulled from a pool pulseless and apneic. Bystanders are trying to push water out of the lungs. What should the paramedic do next?
rationale: C is best because drowning arrest is hypoxic; CPR with early ventilation and oxygenation is the priority. A, B, and D emphasize other arrest tasks while missing early ventilation.

title: Croup Treatment Without Agitation

title: Safe Pediatric Ambulance Restraint
stem: A toddler needs transport after a minor crash. The parent wants to hold the child on the cot during transport. What should the paramedic do next?

options.A: Let the parent hold the child on the cot for comfort
options.B: Secure the child with an appropriate pediatric restraint system
options.C: Use the vehicle seatbelt across the child and parent together
options.D: Place the child on the cot and have an EMT brace the child during turns

rationale: B is best because a child should be secured with an appropriate pediatric restraint system during ambulance transport. A, C, and D use unsafe lap, shared-belt, or manual-holding workarounds.
citation: National Highway Traffic Safety Administration. Working Group Best-Practice Recommendations for the Safe Transportation of Children in Emergency Ground Ambulances. title: Geriatric Head Injury Undertriage
stem: A 78-year-old on anticoagulants fell from standing, struck the head, and is slightly more confused than baseline. What should the paramedic do next?
rationale: C is best because geriatric trauma is prone to under-triage, and anticoagulant use, head impact, and altered baseline justify transport for appropriate trauma evaluation. A treats the visible injury but misses risk. B waits for hypotension. D shifts monitoring to family.

title: Acute Delirium In Dementia

title: Bariatric Respiratory Positioning

title: Hospice Dyspnea Goals Of Care
stem: A hospice patient has severe dyspnea, and no DNR or POLST is immediately available. What should the paramedic do next?

options.C: Transport immediately to the emergency department
options.D: Search for DNR or POLST paperwork

rationale: B is best because hospice enrollment does not mean no EMS care; the crew should clarify goals, provide comfort-focused symptom relief within scope, and verify orders. A, C, and D each delay goal-concordant symptom care.

title: Caregiver Input For Home Ventilator Alarm
stem: A child with a tracheostomy and home ventilator is in respiratory distress. The caregiver says, "This alarm is not the usual one." What should the paramedic do next?

topic: Management of bystanders while caring for patient
title: Bystander History During Ongoing Care
stem: A cyclist is confused after a crash. Several bystanders saw the event, and one crew member is available while the primary assessment continues. What should the crew do next?

options.B: Gather witness history as a crew
options.C: Ask bystanders to search the patient's phone
options.D: Assign one rescuer to gather witness history

rationale: D is best because one rescuer can gather high-value bystander history while primary care continues. A delays history. B overcommits the crew to history gathering. C uses bystanders for a lower-priority task.
citation: Dees L. Family and bystanders. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:546. title: Blast Scene Secondary-Device Safety
stem: A 911 call reports an explosion at a public event, but no safe ingress route has been established. What should arriving EMS do next?

options.D: Send one rescuer to scout route

rationale: A is best because blast scenes can include secondary devices and unsafe routes, so staging and command-coordinated ingress precede triage. B, C, and D commit resources forward without a confirmed safe route.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:283. title: Suspected MPox Source Control
stem: A stable patient with suspected mpox needs transport. What should the paramedic do next?

options.A: Load patient, notify hospital en route
options.B: Use PPE, cover lesions, notify facility
options.C: Ask exposure history, rash onset, contacts
options.D: Document lesions, transport, notify public health

rationale: B is best because suspected mpox EMS care uses PPE, lesion coverage, limited unnecessary contact, and receiving-facility notification. A and D delay protection or facility notification until transport is underway. C gathers exposure history but does not provide source control or notify the receiving facility.
citation: Koenig KL, Bey CK, Marty AM. Monkeypox 2022: A Primer and Identify-Isolate-Inform (3I) Tool for Emergency Medical Services Professionals. Prehosp Disaster Med. 2022;37(5):687-692. Locator: Primary Source Materials/Gap Fill Sources/MPox/Koenig_2022_Mpox_EMS_3I_Prehosp_Disaster_Med.pdf; EMS Identify-Isolate-Inform and PPE/source-control recommendations; PDF p 5.
